message.content: {"schema_version":"stella.literature_hvs_candidates.v0.1","generated_at":"2026-06-13T01:09:50","paper":{"arxiv_id":"1901.04559","bibcode":"2019ApJ...872...58H","title":"Identifying Sagittarius Stream Stars By Their APOGEE Chemical Abundance Signatures","month":"2019-01","source_note_json":"notes/2019/2019-01/2019-01.json","links":{"abs":"https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.04559","pdf":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.04559"}},"inputs":{"paper_dir":"literature/1901.04559","audit_path":"literature/1901.04559/audit.json","catalog_review_path":"literature/1901.04559/catalog_review.json","catalog_extraction_path":"literature/1901.04559/catalog_extraction.json","ecsv_paths":[]},"extraction":{"status":"partial","extracted_at":"2026-06-13T01:09:50","extractor":"agent","summary":"Paper identifies one hypervelocity star candidate (HVS) that is unbound in MWPotential2014. The HVS is selected from the Sgr chemical candidate cluster and found to be unbound based on orbit integration. The APOGEE ID of the HVS is not available in the provided arxiv source (the full Table 1 is omitted).","tooling":null},"method_chain":[{"id":"step-01","depends_on":[],"step_type":"input_catalog","summary":"APOGEE DR14 data release, including stellar parameters, chemical abundances, distances from the value-added catalog, and radial velocities, covering ~176,000 red giant stars.","inputs":["APOGEE DR14 catalog"],"outputs":["APOGEE stellar sample with Teff, logg, [Fe/H], chemical abundances, distances, radial velocities"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":160,"end_line":164,"context":"description of APOGEE sample and DR14"}]},{"id":"step-02","depends_on":[],"step_type":"input_catalog","summary":"Gaia DR2 data for proper motions.","inputs":["Gaia DR2"],"outputs":["proper motion catalog"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":178,"end_line":180,"context":"Gaia DR2 proper motions acquisition"}]},{"id":"step-03","depends_on":["step-01"],"step_type":"quality_filter","summary":"Apply quality cuts to APOGEE sample for chemical clustering: Teff between 3550 and 4200 K, S/N >70, no ASPCAPBAD flag, [Fe/H] > -1.2.","inputs":["APOGEE DR14 sample"],"outputs":["Clean sample of cool red giants"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":165,"end_line":175,"context":"sample definition for clustering"}]},{"id":"step-04","depends_on":["step-03"],"step_type":"sample_selection","summary":"Perform k-means clustering (k=16) in 6-dimensional chemical abundance space ([O/Fe], [(C+N)/Fe], [Mg/Fe], [Al/Fe], [Mn/Fe], [Ni/Fe]) to identify stars with Sgr-like abundance patterns, referred to as Sgr stream candidates. The HVS candidate is one of these chemically selected stars.","inputs":["Chemical abundances of the clean sample"],"outputs":["68 Sgr stream candidates"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":219,"end_line":248,"context":"k-means clustering method and selection of Sgr stream candidates"}]},{"id":"step-05","depends_on":["step-01","step-02"],"step_type":"cross_match","summary":"Cross-match APOGEE stars with Gaia DR2 using nearest-neighbor within 1 arcsec in TOPCAT, obtaining Gaia proper motions for 62 of 68 candidates.","inputs":["APOGEE catalog","Gaia DR2"],"outputs":["Proper motions for Sgr stream candidates"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":179,"end_line":181,"context":"Gaia cross-match"}]},{"id":"step-06","depends_on":["step-01"],"step_type":"distance_estimation","summary":"Adopt spectro-photometric distances from the APOGEE DR14 value-added catalog (NMSU_dist), accurate to ~20%.","inputs":["APOGEE DR14 VAC distances"],"outputs":["distances"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":175,"end_line":176,"context":"distance estimates from DR14 VAC"}]},{"id":"step-07","depends_on":["step-01"],"step_type":"radial_velocity_measurement","summary":"Use radial velocities from APOGEE DR14 spectra as provided by the ASPCAP pipeline.","inputs":["APOGEE spectra"],"outputs":["radial velocities"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":160,"end_line":163,"context":"APOGEE data includes radial velocities"}]},{"id":"step-08","depends_on":[],"step_type":"solar_position_and_motion","summary":"Adopt solar Galactocentric distance R0 = 8.0 kpc, circular velocity Vcirc = 220 km/s, and solar peculiar motion from Schönrich et al. (2010).","parameters":[{"name":"R0","raw_value":"8.0 kpc","value":"8.0","unit":"kpc","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":181,"end_line":181,"context":"solar radius 8.0 kpc"}]},{"name":"v_circ_sun","raw_value":"220 km/s","value":"220","unit":"km s^-1","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":181,"end_line":181,"context":"circular velocity 220 km/s"}]},{"name":"solar_motion_u","raw_value":"as measured by Schönrich+2010","value":"","description":"U component from Schönrich+2010; numeric value not reported in paper","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":181,"end_line":181,"context":"solar motion from Schönrich+2010"}]}],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":181,"end_line":181,"context":"adopted solar position and motion"}]},{"id":"step-09","depends_on":["step-05","step-06","step-07","step-08"],"step_type":"velocity_calculation","summary":"Calculate Galactocentric Cartesian velocities (U, V, W) using Gaia proper motions, distances, radial velocities, and the solar parameters, following a left-handed coordinate system with X toward Galactic center, Y along rotation, Z toward north Galactic pole.","inputs":["proper motions","distances","radial velocities","solar parameters"],"outputs":["Galactocentric velocities (U,V,W) and total space velocity"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":181,"end_line":182,"context":"description of kinematic calculations and coordinate system"}]},{"id":"step-10","depends_on":[],"step_type":"galactic_potential_model","summary":"Use the MWPotential2014 gravitational potential model, a composite of a spheroidal bulge, Miyamoto-Nagai disk, and NFW halo, as implemented in galpy.","parameters":[{"name":"potential_name","raw_value":"MWPotential2014","value":"MWPotential2014","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":181,"end_line":181,"context":"MWPotential2014"}]}],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":181,"end_line":181,"context":"usage of MWPotential2014 from galpy"}]},{"id":"step-11","depends_on":["step-09","step-10"],"step_type":"orbit_integration","summary":"Integrate orbits of the Sgr stream candidates forward and backward for 1.5 Gyr using galpy in MWPotential2014, deriving orbital parameters including eccentricity, apocenter, pericenter, angular momentum.","inputs":["Galactocentric positions and velocities","MWPotential2014"],"outputs":["orbital parameters (eccentricity, rap, rperi, ...)"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":181,"end_line":182,"context":"orbit integration description"}]},{"id":"step-12","depends_on":["step-11","step-10"],"step_type":"escape_or_bound_assessment","summary":"Assess whether each star is bound or unbound. The HVS candidate is found to have no measured apocenter (at the last time step it is ~760 kpc from the Galactic center) and is therefore unbound in MWPotential2014.","inputs":["orbit integration results","MWPotential2014"],"outputs":["bound/unbound status"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":296,"end_line":298,"context":"HVS: no measured apocenter, unbound"},{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":389,"end_line":391,"context":"HVS orbit details and unbound conclusion"}]},{"id":"step-13","depends_on":["step-12"],"step_type":"candidate_classification","summary":"Classify candidates into groups: Sgr stream, accreted halo, bulge, HVS, others. The HVS is identified as a separate category based on its unbound orbit.","inputs":["orbit parameters and bound status"],"outputs":["classification labels"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":290,"end_line":298,"context":"definition of groups including HVS"}]}],"candidates":[{"identifiers":{"record_id":"1901.04559:cand-001","paper_candidate_id":"HVS candidate","gaia_source_id":"","all":[{"value":"HVS candidate","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":296,"end_line":298,"context":"introducing HVS group"}]},{"value":"d","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":321,"end_line":321,"context":"table footnote: d = HVS"}]}]}}],"candidate_groups_considered":[{"group_id":"table-1","description":"Table 1: Sgr Stream Cluster, listing APOGEE IDs with membership assignments (a-e), including the HVS candidate (d).","decision":"included","reason":"The HVS candidate (membership code d) is identified in this table; it is the only object with Galactic unbound status.","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":304,"end_line":325,"context":"caption and partial table of Sgr Stream Cluster"}]}]}

But the actual text describing the solar parameters is in line 181 itself: "Throughout this work, we assume a circular velocity of 220~km~s$^{-1}$ at a Solar radius of 8.0~kpc from the Galactic center, and the Solar motion as measured by \citet{Schoenrich2010}." That line is not a comment. In the provided tex file, line numbers:
181|\item We supplement the APOGEE radial velocities and distance estimates with proper motions from \emph{Gaia} Data Release 2 \citep[DR2;][]{GaiaDR2}. Using the nearest neighbors matching tool within TOPCAT \citep{Topcat}, we identify the best \emph{Gaia} match (nearest neighbor within $1''$) for each APOGEE star. Of the 68 chemical candidates we identify in \S \ref{clust}, 62 have \emph{Gaia} proper motion matches. All but two of the sources matched within 0.4'' (the other two are at 0.73'' and 0.86''). We then use the full 6-D phase-space data (i.e., 3-D positions and velocities) to integrate orbits in a model Galactic potential, and further check the membership of our APOGEE-selected sample in the Sgr stream (described in further detail in \S \ref{res}).
182|
Wait, I need to re-examine the exact line numbers as provided in the file content. The given tex file lines are numbered. Let's look at the passage around the line containing the solar parameters. The agent originally used line 182 for solar references. But we see:
```
181|To further investigate whether we have recovered true Sgr stream stars, we use the proper motions from \emph{Gaia} and the distances and radial velocities from APOGEE to calculate orbits of the Sgr stream candidates. Orbits were integrated in the \texttt{MWPotential2014} potential that is implemented in the \texttt{galpy} \citep{Bovy2015galpy} suite of Python routines\footnote{Available at \url{https://github.com/jobovy/galpy}}. This potential is a composite of a spheroidal bulge, a Miyamoto-Nagai disk, and an NFW halo (see default parameters in \citealt{Bovy2015galpy}). We note that while many recent works suggest that the halo mass of \texttt{MWPotential2014} may be too low to match Milky Way observations \citep[e.g., ][]{Helmi18,Fritz18,Watkins18}, we are mainly interested in the relative orbital properties of stars, so the absolute scale of the potential is unimportant. Throughout this work, we assume a circular velocity of 220~km~s$^{-1}$ at a Solar radius of 8.0~kpc from the Galactic center, and the Solar motion as measured by \citet{Schoenrich2010}.  We follow the \texttt{galpy} usage of a left-handed Galactic Cartesian coordinate (velocity) system with $X (U)$ positive toward the Galactic center, $Y (V)$ along Galactic rotation, and $Z (W)$ in the direction of the north Galactic pole. We initialize the orbit of each star at its current position and velocity, then integrate its orbit both forward and backward for 1.5~Gyr. From this, we estimate the orbital parameters, including the eccentricity, the peri- and apo-center distances ($r_{\rm peri}$ and $r_{\rm apo}$; relative to the Galactic center), and orbital energy and angular momentum.
182|
183|%[[TODO Jeff: Proper Gaia references and where you got the data from]]
184|%We supplement the APOGEE data with proper motions from Gaia...
```
